title: Cade Cunningham Expected To Return From Collapsed Lung Before Playoffs
summary: Cade Cunningham was upgraded to doubtful before being ruled out of Monday night's 123-107 loss by the Detroit Pistons to the Orlando Magic. The move signals the Eastern Conference's top seed could have its star player back before the playoffs begin. Cunningham has been sidelined since March 19 with a collapsed left lung and has since entered a return-to-play protocol. Center Isaiah Stewart, out since March 15 with a left calf strain, was also elevated to doubtful before being ruled out Monday. "Both of them are progressing. They are doing more and more each day," said Pistons coach J.B. Bickerstaff. "With both of these things, it is always about how you respond the next day. I don't want to jump out there and say anything, but so far, so good. They are taking steps in the right direction." Bickerstaff expressed optimism that both Cunningham and Stewart will return before the postseason opens. The Detroit Pistons have three regular season games remaining. Detroit has proven resilient during Cunningham's absence, clinching the No. 1 overall seed in the Eastern Conference with a win over the Philadelphia 76ers on Saturday. The Pistons remain the most dominant team in the East despite losing their primary offensive engine. Before the injury, Cunningham was generating MVP consideration, averaging 24.5 points and 9.9 assists per game. Stewart's availability would also bolster a frontcourt depth the Pistons will need heading into a postseason run.

title: Victor Wembanyama Exits Monday's Game With Rib Injury, Award Eligibility At Risk
summary: The San Antonio Spurs ruled Victor Wembanyama out for the second half of Monday's game against the Philadelphia 76ers due to a left rib contusion. The 22-year-old big man sustained the injury in a collision with 76ers forward Paul George near half-court. The contact occurred with 10:47 remaining in the second quarter as Wembanyama received a pass during a transition sequence. He initially subbed out and went to the locker room before returning to the bench with 6:49 left in the first half. Wembanyama then requested a second substitution with 44.3 seconds before intermission and did not return after halftime. Wembanyama logged 15 minutes and 40 seconds, which will count toward postseason award eligibility. Players receive two exceptions to the standard 20-minute requirement, provided they play at least 15 minutes. He finished with 17 points, five rebounds and three blocks. The runaway favorite for NBA Defensive Player of the Year and a legitimate MVP contender , Wembanyama must play at least 20 minutes in one of San Antonio's final three games to preserve his award eligibility. The injury interrupts a dominant stretch for the French center. He had posted at least 30 points and 15 rebounds with three blocks in three consecutive games, tied for the second-longest such streak since blocks were officially tracked in 1973-74, trailing only Shaquille O'Neal's four-game run in 1999, per ESPN Research. Wembanyama entered Monday leading the San Antonio Spurs in scoring (24.9 points per game), rebounds (11.6) and blocks (3.1).

title: Cooper Flagg Overtakes Kon Knueppel As NBA Rookie Of The Year Favorite After Historic 96-Point Weekend
summary: Cooper Flagg has surged to the top of NBA Rookie of the Year betting markets after a record-setting two-game stretch. The 19-year-old Dallas Mavericks forward totaled 96 points over the weekend, overtaking Kon Knueppel of the Charlotte Hornets as the frontrunner. Knueppel received 80 votes first place votes compared to 20 for Flagg in last week's ESPN straw poll . DraftKings Sportsbook now lists Flagg at -250, with Knueppel dropping to +180. Knueppel had entered the weekend as the -300 favorite, with Flagg at +225. Flagg posted 51 points Friday against the Orlando Magic, a teenage record, then added 45 points, nine assists and eight rebounds in Sunday's 134-128 victory over the Los Angeles Lakers. He became the first rookie since Allen Iverson to average at least 45 points, five rebounds and five assists across a two-game stretch, and the only teenager to accomplish the feat. According to ESPN Research, Flagg is the first rookie to score 45 or more points in back-to-back games since Walt Bellamy in 1962. He now has three of the four 45-point performances by a teenager in NBA history. The five other rookies with three such games, Bellamy, Wilt Chamberlain, Michael Jordan, Earl Monroe and Lew Alcindor, all won Rookie of the Year and are Hall of Famers. "I don't know if he's making a closing statement," said Jason Kidd. "I think he's doing what he's been doing all season. Being able to play different positions. Being able to be uncomfortable. He's never complained and has delivered for us. Tonight, being able to do it on national television, it's not easy. Especially coming off a 50-ball. He wants to win, and he helped the team win tonight." Flagg leads all rookies in scoring at 20.8 points per game and ranks among qualifiers in both rebounding (6.6) and assists (4.5). He is one of only four rookies since the ABA-NBA merger, alongside Larry Bird, Jordan and Luka Doncic, to average at least 20 points, six rebounds and four assists. "He's obviously special," said LeBron James, who scored 30 points, with 15 assists and nine rebounds in defeat. "He's getting better and better and better."

title: Dusty May Commits To Michigan, Won't Leave For North Carolina Job
summary: Michigan head coach Dusty May has told university officials he is not seeking other coaching positions, according to ESPN sources. The move effectively ends any possibility of May landing at North Carolina, which had identified him as an early candidate. May's pursuit was never serious given Michigan's run to Monday's national championship game against UConn. The Wolverines finished 36-3, claimed the Big Ten regular-season title and recorded 29 double-digit victories. "I think it's well-documented how happy I am at Michigan," May said Friday. "Obviously, my private life, my personal life, my family, their happiness is very important." May signed a contract extension last year that runs through 2030. Departing for another program this spring would trigger a $7 million buyout. North Carolina dismissed Hubert Davis last month following five seasons. The Tar Heels surrendered a 19-point lead to VCU in the first round, marking back-to-back first-round exits. Arizona's Tommy Lloyd, considered the primary target for the Chapel Hill post, agreed to a fresh five-year contract to remain in Tucson on Friday. With both Lloyd and May unavailable, Chicago Bulls head coach Billy Donovan is expected to emerge as a leading candidate for the Carolina vacancy.

title: UCLA Beats South Carolina 79-51 For First Women's NCAA Title
summary: UCLA won its first NCAA women's basketball championship Sunday, routing South Carolina 79-51 in the national title game in Phoenix. The 28-point margin ranks as the third largest in Division I women's championship history. The Bruins never trailed, building a double-digit lead after the first quarter and extending their winning streak to 31 games. Lauren Betts was named the Final Four's Most Outstanding Player. "We got whooped. Not beat. We got whooped," South Carolina's Raven Johnson said. Five UCLA players reached double figures. Gabriela Jaquez led all scorers with 21 points, 10 rebounds and five assists. Betts added 14 points and 11 rebounds. South Carolina's three primary contributors, Joyce Edwards, Ta'Niya Latson and Johnson, combined for just 15 points on 5-of-22 shooting. The Gamecocks finished at 29% from the field, their worst shooting performance in tournament history. UCLA opened the second half on a 12-3 run to put the game beyond reach. The victory ended a 48-year wait since the program's 1978 AIAW title, with that era's standout Ann Meyers Drysdale in attendance Sunday. "My dream was to be at UCLA," Jaquez said. "We were determined, the core group, to do something UCLA hadn't done before in the NCAA era. We always believed. Job is finished." Coach Cori Close, in her 15th season leading the program, became the longest-tenured single-school head coach to claim a first NCAA championship. "It's immeasurably more than I could ask or imagine," Close said. "It's beyond my wildest dreams."
